stem: A 22-year-old non-smoking woman attends her GP wanting to start a contraceptive pill. She has regular 28-day cycles and her last period started 5 days ago. She has migraines without aura roughly once a month, well controlled with paracetamol. BP at this visit is 118/72 and BMI 23. She wants the most effective pill option starting today and to know when she can rely on it.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peripheral oedema; cardiovascular exam unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Pregnancy test negative; cervical screening up to date

lead_in: Which approach most appropriately initiates contraception today?

options.text: Defer starting until her next period and recommend condoms for the next three weeks
options.rationale: Quick-start is safe and reduces unplanned pregnancy versus deferral; waiting is no longer routinely recommended. (Source: RACGP contraception 2024)

options.text: Use a progesterone-only pill instead of a combined pill due to her migraine history
options.rationale: Migraine without aura is UKMEC 2 for the combined pill; only migraine with aura is UKMEC 4 (an absolute contraindication). (Source: FSRH UKMEC 2023)

options.text: Prescribe the combined pill but only after she completes 3 months of cycle tracking
options.rationale: Regular cycles are already documented; further tracking delays effective contraception without clinical benefit. (Source: RACGP contraception 2024)

options.text: Insert a copper IUD today as it is the most effective hormone-free contraceptive method
options.rationale: Copper IUD is highly effective but is not what the patient is requesting and is a separate decision-making conversation. (Source: RACGP contraception 2024)

options.text: Start the combined pill today and use back-up contraception for 7 days from today
options.rationale: Quick-start within 5 days of period onset gives immediate cover; many AU guidelines recommend a 7-day back-up to harmonise advice and protect against ovulation if any pills are missed. (Source: RACGP contraception 2024; FSRH UKMEC quick start)

explanation.summary: Quick-start COCP means commencing the pill the day the prescription is given, after excluding pregnancy. If started within the first 5 days of a normal menstrual cycle no back-up is theoretically required, but Australian guidance now standardises a 7-day condom back-up message to simplify counselling and reduce error. Migraine without aura is UKMEC 2 (benefits outweigh risk) for the COCP; migraine with aura, age over 35 with smoking, BP over 160/100, or prior VTE are absolute contraindications.
key_points: Quick-start COCP after excluding pregnancy; back-up condoms for 7 days.
Migraine WITHOUT aura: UKMEC 2 for COCP.
Migraine WITH aura: UKMEC 4 - do not use COCP.
BP >= 160/100, age >= 35 + smoking, or prior VTE = UKMEC 4.
LARC (IUD, implant) remains more effective than COCP and warrants discussion.
explanation.pearls: A new-onset focal aura in a woman on the COCP is a stop-now indication; switch to progesterone-only and arrange neurology review to exclude TIA.

stem: A 27-year-old G3P1 woman at 8+4 weeks by certain dates presents with light fresh vaginal bleeding for 6 hours and lower abdominal cramping. She has no syncope and the bleeding has now slowed. Her previous miscarriage was at 7 weeks two years ago; this pregnancy was planned and otherwise well. She is Rh-positive and her booking bloods are unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/76, HR 78, T 36.6°C, abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Examination: Speculum: closed cervical os, minimal fresh blood in vault, no products visible
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: single intrauterine sac with foetal pole CRL 18 mm, FHR 162; small 12 mm subchorionic haematoma adjacent to sac

lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Bed rest for 7 days with daily progesterone pessaries and weekly ultrasound review
options.rationale: Strict bed rest does not reduce miscarriage and progesterone benefit is restricted to women with prior miscarriages on a different protocol. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy bleeding 2023)

options.text: Admit for 48 hours of intravenous tranexamic acid and crossmatched blood standby
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is not standard in threatened miscarriage with light bleeding and stable vitals; admission is not required. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy bleeding 2023)

options.text: Offer surgical evacuation of the haematoma to reduce ongoing miscarriage risk later
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is not indicated for a small subchorionic haematoma with a viable foetus; it does not improve outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy bleeding 2023)

options.text: Commence vaginal micronised progesterone 400 mg twice daily until 16 weeks routinely
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone has a small benefit in women with prior miscarriage AND current bleeding (PRISM trial); this patient's prior loss was 2 years ago and the benefit is modest with NNT around 13. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy bleeding 2023)

options.text: Reassure, continue normal activities and review with repeat ultrasound in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Viable intrauterine pregnancy at 8 weeks with light bleeding and a small subchorionic haematoma has a good prognosis; expectant management with safety-netting is AU first-line. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy bleeding 2023)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage (vaginal bleeding before 20 weeks with a viable intrauterine pregnancy and closed os) occurs in around 1 in 5 pregnancies; about 50 percent continue without complication. Once a foetal heart is detected by transvaginal ultrasound at 8 to 9 weeks the subsequent miscarriage risk falls to under 5 percent in low-risk women. Subchorionic haematoma confers a modest increase in miscarriage risk but a small haematoma in a viable pregnancy is reassuring. Bed rest, progesterone (except per PRISM in recurrent miscarriage), and surgical intervention have no general benefit.
key_points: Threatened miscarriage = bleeding + closed os + viable IUP before 20 weeks.
After foetal heart on TVUS, ongoing miscarriage risk under 5 percent.
Vaginal progesterone benefit is restricted (PRISM): prior miscarriage AND current bleeding.
Bed rest does not reduce miscarriage rate.
Rh-negative women with bleeding require Anti-D from 12 weeks onwards.
explanation.pearls: Subchorionic haematoma over 50 percent of the sac diameter raises the miscarriage risk substantially; document the haematoma size as a fraction of the sac, not just in millimetres.

stem: A 29-year-old woman is reviewed on day 4 after an emergency caesarean for failure to progress with prolonged rupture of membranes. She reports increasing lower abdominal pain, malodorous lochia and rigors since this morning. She has no penicillin allergy. The wound is clean and dry; her hospital documented an estimated blood loss of 700 mL with no transfusion.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen suprapubic tenderness, uterus 18 cm above pubis (subinvoluted), foul-smelling lochia
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.5 x10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, Hb 96 g/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, blood and high vaginal cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Septic-range temperature and tachycardia after caesarean require IV therapy; oral cover is insufficient for moderate to severe endometritis. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Intravenous ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole until afebrile for 24 hours
options.rationale: AU eTG first-line for moderate to severe postpartum endometritis is ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole until afebrile for 24 to 48 hours, then no oral step-down is typically needed. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam with infectious diseases consult overnight
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum carbapenem-sparing cover is reserved for failure of first-line therapy or known resistant organisms; it is not first-line. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin and oral metronidazole with discharge once two doses given
options.rationale: Cefazolin monotherapy does not cover anaerobes adequately and discharge before clinical improvement risks deterioration. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Single dose intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g with planned obstetric outpatient follow-up tomorrow
options.rationale: Single-dose therapy is inadequate for established polymicrobial endometritis; inpatient combination therapy is required. (Source: eTG)

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis affects up to 6 percent of caesareans (higher with prolonged ROM, intrapartum chorio, multiple vaginal examinations). It is polymicrobial - aerobic and anaerobic Gram-positives and Gram-negatives - so AU first-line combination therapy is ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole IV. Clinical improvement is expected within 48 to 72 hours; persistence beyond that warrants imaging to exclude retained products, pelvic abscess, septic pelvic thrombophlebitis or wound infection.
key_points: Triad: fever, foul lochia, uterine tenderness after delivery.
Polymicrobial - cover Gram-positive, Gram-negative and anaerobes.
AU first-line: ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole IV.
Continue until afebrile 24 to 48 hours; oral step-down is usually not needed.
No improvement at 48 to 72 hours = image for retained products or abscess.
explanation.pearls: Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis presents as ongoing fever despite appropriate antibiotics for endometritis; CT pelvis with venous-phase contrast and therapeutic anticoagulation is diagnostic and therapeutic.

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 36 weeks attends to discuss her birth options. Her first child was born by emergency lower-segment caesarean section for failure to progress at 6 cm dilation, with no intraoperative complications. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated, the foetus is cephalic and growth is on the 50th centile. She wants to know her chances of a successful vaginal birth and the main maternal risk to consider.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78, HR 80, fundal height 36 cm, foetal heart 144
data_table.Examination: Vertex cephalic, longitudinal lie, free above pelvic brim, normal liquor volume
data_table.Investigations: Previous operative note: low transverse uterine incision, no extension, single layer closure

lead_in: Which counselling point is most accurate?

options.text: Her chance of vaginal birth is about 70 percent with uterine rupture risk around 0.5 percent
options.rationale: AU data show VBAC success around 70 percent after one prior LSCS with low transverse incision and uterine rupture risk around 0.5 percent; both are core consent points. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC 2019)

options.text: Her chance of vaginal birth is around 90 percent with uterine rupture risk under 0.1 percent
options.rationale: VBAC success rates with one prior LSCS sit around 60 to 75 percent in AU centres; 90 percent overstates the chance. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC 2019)

options.text: Her chance of vaginal birth is around 40 percent with uterine rupture risk around 2 percent
options.rationale: Both figures are too pessimistic; the actual success and rupture rates after one prior LSCS are more favourable. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC 2019)

options.text: Her chance of vaginal birth is around 70 percent with uterine rupture risk around 4 percent
options.rationale: Success rate is correct but rupture risk after a single low-transverse LSCS is around 0.5 percent, not 4 percent. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC 2019)

options.text: Her chance of vaginal birth is around 50 percent with uterine rupture risk under 0.05 percent
options.rationale: Both rates underestimate AU outcomes; 50 percent under-counsels success and 0.05 percent under-counsels rupture risk. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC 2019)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG VBAC counselling after one previous low-transverse LSCS quotes a 60 to 75 percent vaginal birth success (around 70 percent average), uterine rupture risk around 0.5 percent (rising with induction or augmentation), and increased need for blood transfusion or hysterectomy compared with planned repeat caesarean. Continuous intrapartum CTG and rapid access to caesarean are non-negotiable safety requirements. Two or more prior caesareans, a classical incision, or a previous uterine rupture are usual contraindications to VBAC.
key_points: VBAC after one LSCS: 60 to 75 percent vaginal birth success.
Uterine rupture risk around 0.5 percent (higher with induction/augmentation).
Continuous CTG and immediate-access caesarean theatre required.
Classical incision, previous rupture, two or more prior LSCS = generally contraindicated.
Counsel re emergency hysterectomy and transfusion risks.
explanation.pearls: Induction with prostaglandins after a previous caesarean roughly doubles the rupture risk to about 1 percent; balloon catheter mechanical induction does not carry this excess risk and is the preferred AU method when induction is needed.

subtopic_detail: Induction of labour with unfavourable cervix
stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 at 41+3 weeks attends for planned induction of labour. She has no significant medical history and the pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She declines membrane sweep at this visit and is keen for the gentlest induction option. Foetal movements are normal and she is not contracting. The neonatal cot is available and theatre is staffed for emergencies.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/80, HR 82, T 36.6°C; CTG reactive, no decelerations
data_table.Examination: Vertex, cephalic, head 2/5 palpable, station -3; cervix posterior, firm, 1 cm long, closed (Bishop score 2)
data_table.Investigations: Estimated foetal weight 3850 g (75th centile); amniotic fluid index 9 cm; group B negative at 36 weeks

lead_in: Which induction approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous oxytocin infusion commenced at 1 mU/min titrated to contractions
options.rationale: Oxytocin alone is rarely effective with Bishop under 6; cervical ripening is required first. (Source: RANZCOG induction of labour 2024)

options.text: Artificial rupture of membranes followed by titrated oxytocin infusion within an hour
options.rationale: ARM is technically very difficult with a closed posterior cervix and Bishop 2; ripening is needed before ARM is feasible. (Source: RANZCOG IOL 2024)

options.text: Dinoprostone 10 mg controlled-release pessary intravaginally for ripening over 24 hours
options.rationale: Dinoprostone is an acceptable ripening option but carries higher hyperstimulation and CTG-abnormality rates than balloon and is therefore not the gentlest first-line option. (Source: RANZCOG IOL 2024)

options.text: Expectant management with daily CTG and amniotic fluid index for a further week
options.rationale: Post-term induction by 41+3 to 42+0 weeks reduces perinatal mortality compared with ongoing expectant management (SWEPIS, ARRIVE-postdates); further waiting is not advised. (Source: RANZCOG IOL 2024)

options.text: Insertion of a transcervical balloon (Foley or Cook) catheter for mechanical ripening overnight
options.rationale: With Bishop under 6 and an intact unscarred uterus, mechanical balloon ripening is at least equivalent to PGE2 with lower hyperstimulation risk; AU first-line for unfavourable cervix in many centres. (Source: RANZCOG induction of labour 2024; Queensland IOL guideline)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour

explanation.summary: Induction at 41+0 to 41+3 weeks is recommended in AU to reduce stillbirth without increasing caesarean rates; balloon catheter ripening for an unfavourable cervix (Bishop under 6) is at least as effective as PGE2 with a lower hyperstimulation rate, making it the preferred outpatient or low-risk option. PGE2 (dinoprostone gel or pessary) remains acceptable, particularly in inpatient settings. Oxytocin alone is reserved for favourable cervix or after successful ripening; ARM is added once the cervix admits a finger.
key_points: Bishop under 6 = ripen first; balloon or PGE2.
Balloon has lower hyperstimulation rate than PGE2 with equivalent vaginal-birth success.
Avoid PGE2 in women with previous caesarean (rupture risk).
AU induce by 41+3 to 42+0 weeks to reduce stillbirth.
Oxytocin once cervix favourable or membranes ruptured.
explanation.pearls: Balloon ripening can be performed safely in selected day-stay outpatients; reattend in 12 hours or earlier for contractions, ROM or balloon expulsion, which alone usually achieves Bishop over 6.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour
